agent_outputs.raw: The PR-AUC comparison between baseline and challenger is invalid as presented. Two fundamental confounds make the six-basis-point difference uninterpretable. First, the baseline PR-AUC was computed with 30-day provisional labels while the challenger used 90-day matured labels. This is not a minor detail — the label set itself changed. 90-day labels contain more true fraud cases, which means the PR-AUC against 90-day labels is a different quantity than PR-AUC against 30-day labels. The 0.006 difference may largely or entirely reflect the richer label set, not model improvement. Second, the two evaluation periods differ (Q1 vs. Q2) and a merchant onboarding campaign changed Q2's transaction mix. Both confounds must be eliminated by evaluating both models on the same data with the same labels. A paired bootstrap confidence interval is also required given the small gain at rare prevalence.

The benchmark is broken before we even get to model quality questions. The baseline and challenger were evaluated with different fraud label maturation windows (30-day vs. 90-day) on different quarters with known population shift. Each of these alone would be a serious confound; together they make the six-basis-point PR-AUC difference uninterpretable. The fact that the gain is small and reported without uncertainty makes it worse — at 0.2% fraud prevalence, a 0.006 PR-AUC difference can easily be within the noise floor. The temporal split approach (by week, forward-looking) is the right design for fraud and is not the problem. Everything else needs to be fixed in a corrected evaluation.

Three problems invalidate this comparison. One: mismatched label maturation standards (30-day vs. 90-day). Two: different evaluation quarters with documented population shift. Three: no confidence interval for a small gain at rare prevalence. The key issue is that the team interpreted the label quality improvement as a reason to use 90-day labels for the challenger, without recognizing that this made the baseline comparison invalid. The right approach is to apply 90-day labels to the baseline evaluation on the same quarter and rerun the comparison. The temporal split design is sound and should be preserved. Critique wins.

synthesis: All three agents identify the same three issues and all conclude critique wins. The label maturation mismatch is the primary confound; the different evaluation quarters add a secondary confound; the missing confidence interval compounds both. The temporal split design is correctly identified as sound by all three agents.

etd_constraint.condition: Re-evaluate both models on Q1 with 90-day matured labels; compute paired bootstrap 95% CI for PR-AUC difference
etd_constraint.supports_critique_if: Matched gap below 0.003 or 95% CI crosses zero
etd_constraint.supports_defense_if: Matched gap at least 0.006 with 95% CI excluding zero
etd_constraint.ambiguous_if: Positive gap below 0.006 with CI barely overlapping zero

all_issues_raised: Different evaluation quarters with population shift (all three agents)
Missing confidence interval at rare prevalence (all three agents)
